transcript.text: I'm an automated medical assistant. If this is an emergency, hang up and call 911. Do you consent to continue? (yes/no)
Yes.
What is your main medical concern today?
I have lower abdominal pain.
When did your lower abdominal pain start?
2 days ago.
Can you describe the character and exact location of the pain in your lower abdomen?
It's on the left side and it's a sharp stabbing pain.
Any fever, chills, or nausea since the pain started?
Yes. All of the above.
Any changes in your bowel movements or urinary symptoms like pain or frequency?
No. Neither.
Any vaginal bleeding, discharge, or missed periods?
No.
Any history of similar pain episodes or known medical conditions like kidney stones or diverticulitis?
No.
Any pain or tenderness when you press on your lower abdomen, especially on the left side?
Yes.
Any recent sexual activity or possibility of pregnancy?
Recent ****** activity, but no possibility of pregnancy.
Any vomiting or inability to keep liquids down since the pain started?
No.
Any changes in bowel habits such as diarrhea or constipation, or any blood in your stool?
No.
How old are you? Please say your age in years.
Do you have any medication allergies? If yes, list them. If no, say no.
Penicillin.
What sex were you assigned at birth? Please say male or female.
Female.
Is pregnancy possible? (yes/no/unknown)
No.
Have you ever been told you have kidney disease or significantly reduced kidney function? (yes/no/not sure)
No.
Have you ever been told you have liver disease (such as hepatitis, cirrhosis, fatty liver, or abnormal liver tests)? (yes/no/not sure)
No.

It sounds like you’re experiencing sharp pain in your lower left abdomen along with fever and chills, which may be due to an infection like diverticulitis or another inflammation inside your abdomen. Because I can’t examine you or do tests over the phone, a licensed physician will review your information and may prescribe antibiotics if appropriate. For now, you can take acetaminophen or ibuprofen if you need pain relief and can take them safely. If your pain gets worse, if you develop persistent vomiting, can’t keep liquids down, or have a high fever that doesn’t improve, please seek urgent in-person care right away. If you need an exam or testing, going to an urgent care or emergency department is the best step. A doctor will review your case soon and decide if antibiotics or further testing are needed.
